title: ShapleyPass: Quantifying Higher-Order Interactions Among Compiler Optimization Passes via Shapley Interaction Indices
description: We propose framing compiler pass selection as a cooperative game and applying Shapley Interaction Indices to decompose performance contributions into individual effects, pairwise interactions, and higher-order synergies among LLVM optimization passes. This provides the first principled, interpretable analysis of multi-way pass interactions and yields an interaction-guided pass selection algorithm that exploits discovered synergies.
motivation: Current approaches to the compiler phase-ordering problem model only pairwise pass dependencies (ODG, MiCOMP) or use black-box learning (CompilerDream, Compiler-R1) without explaining why certain pass combinations work. Higher-order interactions — where three or more passes together produce effects none of them individually or in smaller subsets achieve — have never been systematically measured. Understanding these interactions would both advance scientific understanding of compiler optimization and enable more effective pass selection algorithms.
proposed_approach: Frame pass selection as a cooperative game where passes are players and the value function maps pass subsets to IR instruction count reduction. Use the shapiq library to compute Shapley Interaction Indices at orders 1 through 3 (and selectively order 4) for the top 20-25 LLVM optimization passes across benchmark programs. Analyze the interaction structure (variance decomposition, synergy/redundancy detection, cross-program stability). Design an interaction-guided greedy pass selection algorithm that prioritizes synergistic combinations. Evaluate on cBench and PolyBench benchmarks against LLVM default levels, individual-greedy, pairwise-only greedy, random search, and genetic algorithm baselines.

related_work.title: MiCOMP: Mitigating the Compiler Phase-Ordering Problem Using Optimization Sub-Sequences and Machine Learning
related_work.authors: Ashouri, Bignoli, Palermo, Silvano, Kulkarni, Cavazos
related_work.relation: Clusters O3 passes into sub-sequences using ML; limited to within-cluster pairwise ordering. Our work provides principled higher-order interaction measurement.

related_work.title: Efficient compiler optimization by modeling passes dependence
related_work.authors: Gao, Yao, Wang, Liu, Li, Chen, Guo
related_work.relation: Models pairwise source-code and performance dependence via an Optimization Dependence Graph. Our work extends beyond pairwise to higher-order interactions using game-theoretic framework.

related_work.title: GroupTuner: Efficient Group-Aware Compiler Auto-Tuning
related_work.authors: Gao, Yao, Wang, Liu, Li, Chen, Guo
related_work.relation: Groups passes by functional coherence for mutation-based tuning. Our work identifies groups based on measured interaction strength rather than heuristic functional similarity.

related_work.title: CompilerDream: Learning a Compiler World Model for General Code Optimization
related_work.relation: Uses model-based RL to learn a world model of pass effects. Black-box approach without interpretable interaction decomposition.

related_work.title: Compiler-R1: Towards Agentic Compiler Auto-tuning with Reinforcement Learning
related_work.authors: Pan, Lin, Luo, Liu, Yao, Zhang, Xing, Wu
related_work.relation: RL-driven LLM for pass sequence optimization. Does not analyze or exploit pass interaction structure.

related_work.title: The Shapley Taylor Interaction Index
related_work.relation: Introduces the mathematical framework we apply to compiler passes. Original paper targets ML feature attribution.

related_work.title: shapiq: Shapley Interactions for Machine Learning
related_work.authors: Muschalik, Baniecki, Fumagalli, Kolpaczki, Hammer, Hüllermeier
related_work.relation: Provides the computational library we use for efficient Shapley interaction computation.

related_work.title: HyperSHAP: Shapley Values and Interactions for Explaining Hyperparameter Optimization
related_work.relation: Applies Shapley interactions to hyperparameter optimization in ML. Closest methodological analog; our work targets compiler passes rather than ML hyperparameters.

hypothesis: Higher-order interactions (order >= 3) among compiler optimization passes account for a significant fraction (>=10%) of the performance variance unexplained by individual pass effects and pairwise interactions alone, and an interaction-aware pass selection algorithm that explicitly exploits these higher-order synergies will outperform pairwise-only methods on the majority of benchmark programs.

confirmed_if: Third-order Shapley interaction indices are statistically significant for >=30% of pass triples tested
Variance decomposition shows >=10% additional variance explained by order-3+ interactions beyond pairwise
Interaction-guided pass selection outperforms pairwise-only greedy on >=60% of benchmark programs
refuted_if: Pairwise analysis captures essentially all pass interaction structure
Interaction-guided algorithm does not outperform simple individual-greedy selection
success_criteria.value_if_refuted: Demonstrates that pairwise modeling is sufficient for compiler pass selection, validating ODG and similar approaches and saving future researchers from pursuing higher-order models

feasibility.scalability_strategy: Focus on top 20-25 passes; use sampling-based Shapley interaction approximation (KernelSHAP-IQ); parallelize across benchmark programs
